summary.intent: Fix six consistency issues identified by Codex review on PR #680 in the ars-ui repo, covering slider pointer geometry, drag commit state, NaN-aware prop diffing, and aria-errormessage for invalid thumbs.
summary.outcome: All six Codex review issues were addressed with regression tests, implementation fixes, spec updates, and snapshot acceptance. The commit 'Harden slider pointer and commit edge cases' was pushed and a new Codex review pass was requested.

repo: The repo uses `cargo xci-fast` as a local fast gate covering fmt, check, clippy, unit tests, integration, adapter tests, spec snippets, and snapshot count — all 10 gates must pass before pushing.
Codex review is triggered via `gh pr comment <num> --body '@codex review'` and tracked by polling PR reactions (👀 = acknowledged, 👍 = approved) and unresolved GraphQL review threads.
Snapshots are accepted with `cargo insta accept` (scoped to package with `-p ars-components`); unreferenced snapshots are rejected via `cargo insta test --unreferenced=reject`.

Spec files live under `spec/components/input/` (e.g., `spec/components/input/slider.md`) and are validated with `cargo xtask spec validate` and compiled with `cargo test -p xtask --test spec_corpus_compile_snippets`.
Skills/workflow docs are stored in `.agents/skills/` (local) and `~/.codex/plugins/cache/` (global plugin cache).

code.finding: Pointer geometry must be guarded for finite/normalized values before use; non-finite pointer coordinates caused silent incorrect behavior.
Drag commit state needs to be tracked separately so that no-op commits (value unchanged) are not incorrectly emitted as change events.
Prop diffing must be NaN-aware; standard equality checks treat NaN != NaN, causing spurious re-renders when slider value is NaN.
Invalid thumbs require aria-errormessage linkage; this was missing and needed a snapshot update after the fix.

workflow: GitHub GraphQL review thread IDs differ from numeric comment IDs needed for REST reply creation — a flat PR comment timeline fetch is required to map between them.
Polling PR reactions alone is insufficient for detecting new Codex review threads; direct GraphQL thread count queries are needed in parallel.
The Codex review loop can show 👀 (acknowledged) while new unresolved threads already exist — the reaction state lags behind actual review posting.
Background poll processes must be explicitly killed (by PID) before starting new review loops to avoid conflicting state reads.
TDD order matters: regression tests are added first to confirm red state, then fixes are applied to reach green before committing.

friction: Codex reaction state (👀) lagged significantly behind actual review thread posting, causing the poll loop to report no issues while 6 unresolved threads already existed.
GraphQL review-thread IDs are incompatible with REST reply-creation endpoints, requiring an extra fetch step to resolve numeric comment IDs.
The reaction-based polling loop ran for an extended period (~30+ intervals) without detecting the new threads, requiring manual intervention to kill the process and switch to direct thread querying.
Commit hook added latency before each push, requiring explicit waits before the push command could proceed.
open_items: A follow-up Codex review pass was requested but not yet completed at session end — approval or further issues remain pending.

The polling strategy for Codex review detection is fragile (reaction lag vs. thread reality); a more robust approach querying threads directly on every poll cycle would prevent the silent-miss scenario encountered here.
